concepts.title: Isomerism in Coordination Compounds
concepts.description: Coordination compounds exhibit two types of isomerism - structural isomerism and stereoisomerism. Structural isomerism arises due to the difference in the arrangement of ligands, while stereoisomerism arises due to the different arrangement of atoms in space.

concepts.title: Importance of Coordination Compounds
concepts.description: Coordination compounds have a wide range of applications in various fields, including medicine, catalysis, and photography.

concepts.title: Coordination Number
concepts.description: The coordination number of a central metal atom or ion is the number of donor atoms or ligands directly bonded to it.

concepts.title: IUPAC Nomenclature
concepts.description: To remember IUPAC nomenclature of coordination compounds, use the phrase 'Ligands, Central Metal, and Count' to represent the ligands, central atom, and the number of ligands.

concepts.title: Difference between Isomers
concepts.description: Students often confuse structural and stereoisomers. To avoid this, remember that structural isomers differ in the arrangement of ligands, while stereoisomers differ in the arrangement of atoms in space.

concepts.title: Types of Ligands
concepts.description: There are two main types of ligands - monodentate and polydentate. Monodentate ligands have one donor atom, while polydentate ligands have multiple donor atoms.

concepts.title: Chelating Ligands
concepts.description: Chelating ligands are polydentate ligands that form multiple bonds with the central metal atom or ion, resulting in a stable complex.

concepts.title: Coordination Compounds in Biological Systems
concepts.description: Coordination compounds play a crucial role in biological systems, including the transport of oxygen in the blood and the action of certain enzymes.
